0101.title: ANNUAL MRO EDUCATION ON PES PROVISIONS

quotes.text: Commanders and Officers-In-Charge (OICs) must ensure that all reporting officials and Marines (sergeants and above) under their charge are well versed in the spirit and intent of this Order.

The key to preserving and improving the quality of performance evaluations is knowledge and understanding of PES policy and Marine Corps values. This is achieved through the education of all reporting officials. Effective training considers the audience, timing of the training in relation to the evaluation or promotion board cycles, and the appropriate topic.

quotes.citation: MCO 1610.7B, Chapter 8, paragraph 4.a. (Performance Evaluation System (PES) Policy)
quotes.text: Commanders shall ensure that RSs, ROs, and SEAs have access to, and understand, all current PES directives.

Commanders shall ensure that all MROs and reporting officials are knowledgeable of applicable provisions of the PES and will conduct appropriate training annually. Commanders shall consider the audience, timing, topic relationship.

Annually educate individual Marines and reporting officials on the contents of this Order, and reinforce the concept that every Marine has a duty and a responsibility to periodically review their MBS and OMPF.

regulatory.summary: The command must conduct annual PES training for all MROs covering chapters 2, 3, and 4 of MCO 1610.7B, with focus on MROW submission and MBS audits.

actions: Ask for the unit's annual PES training records
Verify training occurred within the previous 12 months
Coverage required for all MROs (sergeants and above)
howToInspect.callout: No annual MRO PES training documentation

actions: Interview 3 to 5 MROs from various sections
Ask basic PES knowledge questions:

What is in section A of your fitness report?
How often do you conduct an MBS audit?
What chapters of the PES apply to you as an MRO?

0103.title: ANNUAL RO EDUCATION ON PES PROVISIONS

quotes.text: Commanders and Officers-In-Charge (OICs) must ensure that all reporting officials and Marines (sergeants and above) under their charge are well versed in the spirit and intent of this Order.

quotes.citation: MCO 1610.7B, Chapter 8, paragraph 4.a. (Performance Evaluation System (PES) Policy)
quotes.text: Commanders shall ensure that RSs, ROs, and SEAs have access to, and understand, all current PES directives.
Commanders shall ensure that all MROs and reporting officials are knowledgeable of applicable provisions of the PES and will conduct appropriate training annually.

As the critical link in the reporting chain and the key to the viability of the PES, ROs must provide the experienced leadership, supervision, and detached point of view necessary to ensure consistent, accurate, and unbiased evaluations. ROs ensure adherence to policy and, as the last officer/supervisor in the normal reporting chain, are responsible for all subordinate performance evaluation activities.

Annually educate individual Marines and reporting officials on the contents of this Order, and reinforce the concept that every Marine has a duty and a responsibility to periodically review their MBS and OMPF.

regulatory.summary: The command must conduct annual PES training for all ROs covering chapters 1, 2, 3, 4, and 8, with focus on Section A through C supervision, Section D through H review of RS markings, high mark and adverse justification requirements, Section I review, and RO profile review.

0104.title: ANNUAL ADVERSE REPORT PROCEDURES TRAINING

quotes.text: Commanders and Officers-In-Charge (OICs) must ensure that all reporting officials and Marines (sergeants and above) under their charge are well versed in the spirit and intent of this Order.

Reporting officials must accurately document substandard performance or adjudicated misconduct via adverse fitness reports to ensure that future selection boards only select the most qualified Marines. Reporting officials must demonstrate courage, resolve, and attention to detail in order to timely and accurately complete adverse reports. Most reporting officials write very few adverse fitness reports. It is crucial that all reporting officials, but especially those who have little or no experience writing adverse fitness reports, review this chapter in its entirety to ensure the accurate completion of adverse reports. Prior to preparing an adverse report, the RS, RO and 3OS should review this chapter to ensure that they understand the definitions, responsibilities, and process contained herein.
Commanders shall ensure that all MROs and reporting officials are knowledgeable of applicable provisions of the PES and will conduct appropriate training annually.
Annually educate individual Marines and reporting officials on the contents of this Order.

regulatory.summary: The command must conduct annual training for all reporting officials covering Chapter 5 of MCO 1610.7B in its entirety.

Verify training occurred within the previous 12 months
Coverage required for all reporting officials (RSs, ROs, 3OSs)

0105.title: DC AND GC REPORTS FOR NJP AND COURT-MARTIAL ACTIONS

quotes.text: Submit a GC report when a Marine is promoted, frocked, reverted to enlisted grade, or reduced. (1) The ending date of the report is the actual day the action occurs, not the date of rank recorded for pay and lineal purposes. (2) Submit a GC report for grade reductions resulting from Non-Judicial Punishment (NJP). The ending date of the report is the date the NJP occurred. However, reporting officials will not submit the report in APES until the appeal process is complete. (3) Submit a report for grade reductions resulting from summary, special, or general court-martial action when the Convening Authority (CA) approves the sentence or when automatic reduction provisions set forth in reference (h) are effected.

quotes.citation: MCO 1610.7B, Chapter 3, paragraph 4.b. (Directed by the Commandant of the Marine Corps (DC))
quotes.text: Submit a DC report when significant administrative, commendatory, or adverse action by the MRO occurs that requires immediate reporting to CMC. Additionally, HQMC will use DC reports for administrative fillers to remove date gaps from a Marine's records. (1) The report must cover the period from the end date of the MRO's last report to the date the action occurred. (2) The RS must consider the entire evaluation period when preparing the report, not just the action that caused the report.
Report all disciplinary action via a GC or DC report per the provisions of chapter 3, paragraph 4b and this chapter.

regulatory.summary: The command must submit a GC or DC report for every Marine subject to NJP, court-martial, or other significant adverse action requiring immediate reporting to CMC.

actions: Obtain the command's NJP records for the previous 12 months
Obtain the command's court-martial records for the previous 12 months
Request a letter from the SJA if no records exist

For other significant adverse action: Report submitted as DC, period covers from end of last report to date of action
howToInspect.callout: Report type incorrect (DC vs GC mismatch)

Verify directed comments are present per Chapter 5
howToInspect.callout: Section I comments inadequate for adverse basis

0106.title: QUARTERLY CTR MAINTENANCE

quotes.text: This report provides commanders with specific information on the status of all fitness reports within their unit, sorted by reporting official. Note: If the MRO forwards a completed MROW to the RS, the fitness report will not display on this report until the RS starts the fitness report. Commands shall request the CTR at least quarterly, and shall maintain the reports on file for a minimum of 12 months. The CTR can be requested from the CMC (MMRP-31) by email at smb.manpower.mmrp-31@usmc.mil.

regulatory.summary: The command must request the CTR at least quarterly and maintain copies on file for a minimum of 12 months.

Quarters should be approximately evenly spaced

0107.title: CTR USAGE TO REDUCE LATE AND OUTSTANDING REPORTS

quotes.text: Timely receipt of fitness reports by HQMC ensures complete and accurate updates of Marines' OMPFs. Fitness reports are due within 30 days of the end of the reporting period for normal reports or 60 days for adverse reports. As a leadership responsibility, reporting officials and commanders must ensure timely submission of fitness reports. For late reports, see chapter 8, paragraph 12.

Notify non-complaint subordinate reporting officials of their delinquency and enforce compliance with this order. Notification in and of itself does not absolve the commander of the responsibility to ensure that non-complaint reporting officials gain compliance. Commanders shall take action to reduce the number of delinquent fitness reports and record discrepancies of Marines within their command.
This report provides commanders with specific information on the status of all fitness reports within their unit, sorted by reporting official.

regulatory.summary: The commander must use the CTR to identify delinquent reporting officials, enforce compliance, and reduce the number of late and outstanding fitness reports.

Identify reporting officials with late or outstanding reports

Ask for evidence of commander action on delinquent reports
Look for written notifications, emails, counseling records
Check for tracking documents

Commands shall pull the Missing Last Annual report at least quarterly, and shall maintain the reports on file for a minimum of 12 months.

0110.title: PROACTIVE EFFORTS ON RECORD DISCREPANCIES

quotes.text: The parent command for Active Component and SMCR Marines will assist these individuals in auditing and correcting their performance records.

Annually educate individual Marines and reporting officials on the contents of this Order, and reinforce the concept that every Marine has a duty and a responsibility to periodically review their MBS and OMPF.

Assist the MRO's efforts to locate his or her reporting officials to seek missing fitness reports and for administrative corrections. Request assistance from the CMC (MMRP-31) if reporting officials do not respond within 30 days.
Correct header data on the MBS as required.

Notify non-complaint subordinate reporting officials of their delinquency and enforce compliance with this order. Notification in and of itself does not absolve the commander of the responsibility to ensure that non-complaint reporting officials gain compliance. Commanders shall take action to reduce the number of delinquent fitness reports and record discrepancies of Marines within their command.

regulatory.summary: The command must take proactive action on identified record discrepancies, including notifying affected Marines, assisting with locating reporting officials, and processing administrative corrections.

0111.title: FITNESS REPORT INVENTORY RECORD LOG

quotes.citation: MCO 1610.7B, Chapter 9, paragraph 5.f.(2) (Fitness Report Inventory (Check Your Fitness Report Status))
quotes.text: This report displays all fitness reports received by an individual Marine and highlights any date gaps and overlapping reporting periods. Commands shall pull the individual fitness report inventory for every E-5 through O-5 during the PCS/PCA check-in process. Commands must maintain a record log annotating which Marine the report was pulled for, and the date it was pulled. Additionally, the record log shall be maintained for a minimum of 12 months. In lieu of a separate tracking database, commands may use check-in sheets as the record log, provided that the check-in sheet has a designated spot for, 'Fitness Report Inventory.'

regulatory.summary: The command must pull the individual Fitness Report Inventory for every E-5 through O-5 during PCS/PCA check-in and maintain a record log for a minimum of 12 months.

actions: Ask for the Fitness Report Inventory record log
Accept formats: Excel spreadsheet, logbook, check-in sheet with designated spot, or other tracking database
howToInspect.callout: No fitness report inventory record log

actions: Marine name
Date the inventory was pulled
Identification of who conducted the pull
Pull the command's check-in roster for the previous 12 months
Filter for E-5 through O-5
Verify each Marine on the check-in roster appears in the record log
howToInspect.callout: Log not reconciled with check-in roster

actions: 12 months of coverage required
All Marines who checked in within the previous 12 months represented
Spot check 5 to 10 entries
Ask to see the actual Fitness Report Inventory for those Marines
Verify the inventory matches the record log entry
If using check-in sheets, verify a designated 'Fitness Report Inventory' spot exists
Verify the spot is being filled in for every E-5 through O-5 check-in

Each missing entry is a DISCREPANCY

0201.title: REPORTING CHAIN ESTABLISHMENT WITHIN 30 DAYS

quotes.citation: MCO 1616.1, Chapter 1, paragraph 6.a (Marine Reported On (MRO) Checks in to Unit)
quotes.text: The evaluation cycle begins when the MRO checks into a new unit. The MRO is assigned a reporting chain appropriate to the unit type via permissions management by the battalion and company administrative chiefs. This reporting chain must include an Approver and one other reporting chain role at a minimum.

quotes.text: Once the MRO has an established reporting chain, the FLS or first leader in the MRO's reporting chain will conduct an initial written counseling within 30 days with the MRO and establish billet expectations.

Provide the MRO an initial written counseling within 30 days of the relationship being established, defining the MRO's billet description, role in the unit, responsibilities, and performance expectations.

regulatory.summary: The reporting chain must be established at check-in with a minimum of an Approver plus one other reporting chain role, and the initial counseling must occur within 30 days of the relationship being established.

0202.title: JEPES OCCASION TYPES AND EFFECTIVE DATES

quotes.text: All Marines fall under the JEPES for performance evaluation from the time they enter Recruit Training until they attain the rank of sergeant. Understanding and adherence to the JEPES reporting occasions ensures that there are no gaps in a Marine's record. JEPES facilitates this by auto-preparing Semi-Annual and Annual JEPES worksheets and notifying the reporting chain of upcoming occasions and due dates. However, timely completion of reports is ultimately the responsibility of the O-5 level commander.

Below are the JEPES reporting occasions, their associated 'TO' dates, and other pertinent information. Unless noted otherwise, the 'FROM' date will always be the day following the last day of the previous occasion. In the event that two or more occasions occur at the same time, the higher occasion on the list will be utilized.

regulatory.summary: PR (Promotion): Complete an evaluation when the MRO is promoted to the rank of Corporal or Sergeant. TO DATE: The day prior to the MRO's new date of rank. Promotion to PFC and LCpl do not qualify as a reporting occasion. TR (Transfer): Complete an evaluation when an MRO is transferred externally to another RUC/MCC or temporary RUC/MCC. TO DATE: The MRO's date of transfer. CD (Change of Primary Duty): Complete when MRO has a significant change of duty. TO DATE: Date prior to the date of change of duty. TD (To TAD): Complete when MRO is going TAD for more than 30 days. TO DATE: The MRO's date of departure to TAD. TC (TAD Complete): Complete when MRO returns from TAD of more than 30 Days. TO DATE: The MRO's date of departure from TAD site. AN (Annual): For Reserve Component Marines. FROM DATE: 1 January. TO DATE: 31 December. SA (Semi-Annual): Every six months. FROM DATE: 1 February/1 August. TO DATE: 31 July/31 January. AT (Completion of Annual Training): For Reserve Component. FROM DATE: First day of annual training. TO DATE: Last day of annual training. DC (Discharge): TO DATE: Day before discharge. RD (Reduction): TO DATE: Last day at previous rank. DD (Declared Deserter): TO DATE: First day Marine in Deserter Status. RT (Active Duty Operational Support): FROM DATE: First day of ADOS. END DATE: Last day of ADOS. The unit must use the correct occasion code for each reporting event and apply the correct FROM and TO dates per Chapter 2, paragraph 2.

actions: Access JEPES through MOL
Pull a sample of 10 to 15 completed worksheets from the previous 6 months
Include a mix of occasion types
PR for promotions to Corporal or Sergeant only
TR for transfers to another RUC/MCC
CD for significant change of primary duty
TD for TAD over 30 days
TC for return from TAD over 30 days
AN for Reserve Component annual
SA for active duty semi-annual
AT for Reserve Component annual training completion
DC for discharges
RD for reductions in rank
DD for declared deserter
RT for ADOS completion
PR: day prior to new date of rank
TR: date of transfer
CD: date prior to change of duty
TD: date of departure to TAD
TC: date of departure from TAD site
AN: 31 December
SA: 31 July or 31 January
DC: day before discharge
RD: last day at previous rank
howToInspect.callout: TO date inaccurate on sampled worksheet

actions: FROM date is day following last day of previous occasion (unless specified otherwise)
AN: 1 January
SA: 1 February or 1 August
Verify no date gaps between consecutive occasions

0203.title: JEPES PERMISSIONS AND ORGANIZATIONAL SCOPE MANAGEMENT

quotes.text: Marines fulfilling the role of a MOL manager at the battalion/squadron and company level have administrative permissions to assign Marines roles and permissions, establish reporting chains, and assist their commanders in the management of the JEPES in order to ensure that all Marines are properly evaluated.

The SER is an enlisted leader (E6 or above) who reviews JEPES Worksheets and command marks for accuracy at the platoon and company levels. The SER provides the FLS, Evaluator, and Reviewer comments and recommendations, however does not have the ability to submit draft marks, return reports, or stop the flow of the evaluation through the reporting chain. An SER could be the platoon sergeant, first sergeant, or any SNCO in the unit.

The Command Reviewer is an enlisted leader appointed by the Approver (O-5 level commander or OIC equivalent) who reviews JEPES Worksheets and command marks for accuracy at the battalion/squadron level. The Command Reviewer provides the Reviewer and Approver comments and recommendations, however does not have the ability to submit draft marks, return reports, or stop the flow of the evaluation through the reporting chain. The Command Reviewer could be the command sergeant major or any SNCO in the unit.

regulatory.summary: The unit must manage JEPES permissions through MOL managers at the appropriate organizational levels and ensure SERs and Command Reviewers are properly designated within their organizational scopes.

Verify managers exist at both battalion/squadron and company levels

0204.title: INITIAL COUNSELING WITHIN 30 DAYS

quotes.citation: MCO 1616.1, Chapter 1, paragraph 6.b (MRO's First Line Supervisor Conducts Initial Counseling)
quotes.text: Once the MRO has an established reporting chain, the FLS or first leader in the MRO's reporting chain will conduct an initial written counseling within 30 days with the MRO and establish billet expectations. At this point the MRO and FLS will validate objective scores and initiate any corrective action.

The FLS, as defined in Chapter 1, has direct supervision over the MRO and is the first person in the reporting chain to provide recommended command input marks. The FLS shall: (1) Provide the MRO an initial written counseling within 30 days of the relationship being established, defining the MRO's billet description, role in the unit, responsibilities, and performance expectations. (2) Review the MRO's objective scores in order to ensure accuracy. This includes ensuring that the appropriate waiver is reported in MCTFS for Marines who rate exemptions (Medical, Combat, Unit Waiver) for Rifle, PFT, and CFT, bringing forward a previous score for calculation. (3) Provide recommended subjective command marks and comments/justifications as required in JEPES.

regulatory.summary: The First Line Supervisor must provide an initial written counseling within 30 days of the reporting relationship being established. The counseling must address billet description, role, responsibilities, and performance expectations.

0205.title: JEPES OCCASION APPROVAL TIMELINESS

quotes.text: JEPES reporting occasions must be completed and approved by the end of the reporting period in order to ensure timely and accurate evaluations. The reporting chain may begin forwarding recommended command input marks 45 days prior to the end of the reporting period. Occasions not approved by the end of the reporting period are considered delinquent and will be captured in command reporting.
Commanders have until January 31st and July 31st to complete their SA evaluations respectively, and until December 31st to complete AN evaluations.

For occasions outside of AN and SA which are unknown until relatively shortly before they occur, for example an unexpected TR or PR occasion, the command shall make every effort to complete the evaluation by the end date. Commands are still required to complete transfer occasions even after the Marine physically leaves the unit and falls under the gaining command's accountability.

Timeliness of evaluations are tracked in the JEPES both by the command and Headquarters Marine Corps. Delinquent JEPES worksheets will be reported within a RUC/MCC every 15 days and externally every 30 days.

regulatory.summary: For periodic occasions (AN and SA), evaluations must be completed by the published deadlines. For non-periodic occasions that are unknown until shortly before they occur, including NJP-triggered RD occasions, unexpected TR or PR occasions, and DC occasions, the command must make every effort to complete the evaluation by the end date.

0206.title: APPROVAL AUTHORITY AND ACTING LETTER

quotes.text: The Approver is the O-5 level commander or OIC equivalent in the MRO's chain of command who is responsible for setting and approving final command input marks, thereby certifying the JEPES worksheet for inclusion in the promotion process. As a commander and representative of the CMC, the Approver must ensure reporting officials uphold JEPES policy and protect against inflation. The Approver should only delegate approval authority to one trusted leader in special cases when not able to perform duties. This delegation of approval authority must be documented via an official appointment letter.

The Approver, as defined in Chapter 1, is the O-5 level commander or OIC equivalent who has final approval authority for JEPES worksheets. The Approver is ultimately responsible for the accuracy of JEPES worksheets and the integrity of the system. The Approver may, if unable to perform duties, delegate permissions to perform the below tasks to one trusted leader via appointment letter. However, the Approver always holds ultimate responsibility. The Approver shall: (1) Ensure an MRO's command input marks are accurate and made in accordance with this Order. (2) Input 'not recommended for promotion' as required with every reporting occasion. (3) Meet the timelines set forth in this Order for approving JEPES worksheets. (4) Ensure all Marines have a reporting chain and are receiving performance evaluations. (5) Ensure all reporting chain members are fulfilling their responsibilities in accordance with this Order.

regulatory.summary: The Approver must be the O-5 level commander or OIC equivalent. Delegation of approval authority is allowed only in special cases when the Approver is unable to perform duties, and must be documented via an official appointment letter.

0207.title: COMMAND CORRECTIONS PROCESS

quotes.text: Corrective procedures for JEPES worksheets fall into three situations: those in which late-run objective scores or command input marks could result in an automatic remedial promotion, those in which previously approved command input marks must be corrected, or administrative changes to a JEPES worksheet that do not impact the MRO's PES Score.

The MRO's PES score will automatically recalculate when objective scores and command input marks are reported late in MCTFS. If that new PES score meets or exceeds the cutting score applicable to the month of the late score's effective date, the command will be notified in JEPES that the Marine rates a remedial promotion in accordance with MCO P1400.32D, reference (g).

Previously approved command input marks may need to be changed due to either human error on the part of the reporting chain or unexpected circumstances. (1) If the Approver is still in the MRO's reporting chain, the Approver may directly correct the command input markings of the occasion or return to a member of the reporting chain to re-evaluate. If returned to a member of the reporting chain, the occasion is delinquent until final marks are approved by the Approver. (2) If the Approver for the occasion requiring correction is not still in the MRO's reporting chain, the MRO's current Approver may delete the command input marks as erroneous and remove them from the MRO's average in grade. With supporting documentation, appropriate command input marks may be replaced.

Administrative changes to JEPES worksheets that do not impact an MRO's PES score. For example, a change to the occasion type or 'to date,' are conducted at the battalion/squadron level and are routed to the Approver.

regulatory.summary: The unit must be able to process three categories of corrections: late objective score recalculations resulting in remedial promotion, command input mark corrections, and administrative changes to occasion type or TO date.

Sort examples into the three categories

For corrections by new Approver: verify supporting documentation exists for replacement marks

0208.title: HIGH AND LOW COMMAND INPUT MARK JUSTIFICATIONS

quotes.text: Only those Marines who have displayed the highest levels of Individual Character, MOS and/or Missions accomplishment, and Leadership should be marked 'Exceptional' between 4.1 and 5.0. High marks in any category require a justification comment from the drop-down list of directed comments in JEPES, supported by formal commendatory material visible in JEPES under the Adversity/Commendatory display. Marking a Marine in the 'Exceptional' range should be a rarity and may apply only to a select handful of Marines throughout an annual or semi-annual reporting period.

Marines in the category of 'Below Expectations' fall between 0.1 and 0.9 when the MRO struggles in any of the three categories as evidenced by counseling, documented or informal, that occurred during the reporting period. Marking an MRO in this category requires a justification comment from the drop-down list of directed comments in JEPES, however is not adverse and does not by itself 'NOT REC' the MRO for promotion.

The purpose of the Command Input is to complete the whole picture of the MRO's performance. As the fourth pillar of JEPES equally weighted with Warfighting, Physical Toughness, and Mental Agility, it is the Commander's voice to break out those Marines most or least deserving of promotion.

regulatory.summary: High marks (4.1 to 5.0) require a justification comment from the JEPES drop-down list AND supporting formal commendatory material visible in JEPES. Low marks (0.1 to 0.9) require a justification comment from the JEPES drop-down list and must be supported by counseling that occurred during the reporting period.
